coredata.dc:title: Structure and performance insights in carbon dots-functionalized MXene-epoxy ultrathin anticorrosion coatings
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Jiheng
Zhao, Hongran
Yu, Haibin
coredata.dc:description: MXene nanosheets have showed potential application in metal protection fields due to their large specific surface area and superior mechanical properties. However, MXene for anticorrosion is still underdeveloped, and some associated matters involving susceptibility to oxidation as well as corrosion-promotion activity derived from high conductivity need to be solved. Herein, we use carbon dot (CD) to functionalize Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets via Ti-O-C bonding to obtain CD-Ti3C2Tx hybrids, which significantly improves the chemical stability of MXene in water. Then a flow-induced approach is introduced to construct CD-Ti3C2Tx reinforced epoxy (EP) composite coatings, in which CD-Ti3C2Tx is self-aligned to form an oriented layered structure. The unique structure endows the EP coatings with outstanding anticorrosion performance, and more than 4 orders improvement in impedance modulus at an ultrathin thickness of ∼ 25 μm, and 2 orders much higher than random-dispersed composites with the same fillers content. Besides, the CD also provides additional passivation effect to efficiently suppress crack and local damage propagation. More importantly, the parallel arrangement CD-Ti3C2Tx platelets can inhibit the corrosion-promotion activity by eliminating connections of MXene/MXene and MXene/metal in vertical direction, which will further improve the stability and durability of coatings. This work provides a novel strategy to realize chemically stable MXene and inhibit the corrosion-promotion activity of MXene-based coatings, which possess a combination of anticorrosion performance and passivation effect that are suitable for heavy-anticorrosion applications in harsh sea environment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of CD-Ti3C2TX composites (a). TEM images of MXene (b), CD (c), and CD-Ti3C2TX (d). AFM images of MXene (e), CD (f), and CD-Ti3C2TX (g). SEM images of accordion-like MXene (h), MXene nanosheets (i) and CD-Ti3C2TX (j) after storage for 5 weeks at ambient temperature, the insets in i and j show the corresponding dispersions with a concentration of 1 mg/mL.

Reaction mechanism between CD and MXene (a). FTIR spectra (b), XPS Ti 2p spectra of CD-Ti3C2TX and MXene (c), and C 1 s spectra of CD and CD-Ti3C2TX (d). UV–vis spectra of CD, MXene and CD-Ti3C2TX (e). Raman spectra (f) and XRD pattern (g) of MXene and CD-Ti3C2TX. Cross-sectional SEM images of EP (h), B-CM-EP (i), and R-CM-EP (j) coatings. TEM images of EP (k), B-CM-EP (l), and R-CM-EP (m) ultrathin section coatings. The inset in l is the SEM image of natural nacre.

SEM images of EP (a), R-CM-EP (b), and B-CM-EP (c) coatings, insets show the corresponding contact angles. EIS Bode-Phase (d-f) and Bode-Modulus (g-i) plots at different times of coatings coated steels: EP coating (d and g), R-CM-EP coating (e and h), and B-CM-EP coating (f and i), respectively. Coating resistance R c fitted at different times in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution (j). Comparison of Coating resistance R c of B-CM-EP coating with that of other composite coatings (k): recently reported graphene-, BN-, and Xene-based composite coatings.

The water absorption Wup (a) and breakpoint frequency f b (b) values at different times of coatings. Raman spectra of steels protected by EP, R-CM-EP, and B-CM-EP coatings (c). SEM images and corresponding Raman mapping of steel surfaces protected by EP coating (d), R-CM-EP coating (e), and B-CM-EP coating (f) after 35 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions. The corresponding chemical and electrochemical corrosion reactions of steels under different conditions (g).

Tafel plots of scratched EP (a), R-CM-EP (b), and B-CM-EP (c) coatings in 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions. Plots of tafel parameters of corrosion potential (d) and corrosion current (e) plotted at different times of scratched coatings. Impedance Nyquist plots for scratched EP, R-CM-EP, and B-CM-EP coatings after 48 h of immersion (f), lines are the fitting curves. SEM images of scratched coating protected steels: EP (g), R-CM-EP (h), and B-CM-EP (i), respectively. Corrosion mechanisms of EP (j), R-CM-EP (k), and B-CM-EP (l) coatings.
Digital photographs of neutral salt spray test of steels protected by EP coating (a1-a4), R-CM-EP coating (b1-b4), and B-CM-EP coating (c1-c4) at different tested times.

head.ce:title: Structure and performance insights in carbon dots-functionalized MXene-epoxy ultrathin anticorrosion coatings

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies, Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies, Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: MXene nanosheets have showed potential application in metal protection fields due to their large specific surface area and superior mechanical properties. However, MXene for anticorrosion is still underdeveloped, and some associated matters involving susceptibility to oxidation as well as corrosion-promotion activity derived from high conductivity need to be solved. Herein, we use carbon dot (CD) to functionalize Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets via Ti-O-C bonding to obtain CD-Ti3C2Tx hybrids, which significantly improves the chemical stability of MXene in water. Then a flow-induced approach is introduced to construct CD-Ti3C2Tx reinforced epoxy (EP) composite coatings, in which CD-Ti3C2Tx is self-aligned to form an oriented layered structure. The unique structure endows the EP coatings with outstanding anticorrosion performance, and more than 4 orders improvement in impedance modulus at an ultrathin thickness of ∼ 25 μm, and 2 orders much higher than random-dispersed composites with the same fillers content. Besides, the CD also provides additional passivation effect to efficiently suppress crack and local damage propagation. More importantly, the parallel arrangement CD-Ti3C2Tx platelets can inhibit the corrosion-promotion activity by eliminating connections of MXene/MXene and MXene/metal in vertical direction, which will further improve the stability and durability of coatings. This work provides a novel strategy to realize chemically stable MXene and inhibit the corrosion-promotion activity of MXene-based coatings, which possess a combination of anticorrosion performance and passivation effect that are suitable for heavy-anticorrosion applications in harsh sea environment.

ce:section.ce:para: Metallic corrosion is usually triggered by aggressive species including water, oxygen and chloride ions in harsh conditions, which has become one of the largest challenges facing the steel industry [1]. Metallic corrosion is estimated to be annual direct cost of $2.5 trillion in global $300 billion in the United States, reported from National Association of Corrosion Engineers (NACE) in 2013 [2]. Therefore, it is extremely needed robust and reliable approaches to address metallic corrosion issues. Organic coatings anticorrosion is always regarded as a facile and efficient strategy to against metal corrosion due to its economic and workability features [3-5]. However, organic coatings also display some limitations. They can only provide inferior barrier protection and do not withstand more complex and harsh corrosion attacks [6]. Besides, organic coatings are generally thicker, which may potentially affect the physical properties of the underlying metals [7]. One fascinating method to improve the anticorrosion performance of organic coatings is to embed two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials, for instance graphene [8,9], graphene oxide (GO) [5,6], boron nitride (BN) [10], transition metal dichalcogenides (MoS2 and WS2) [11], layered hydrotalcite (LDH) [12], and a combination of various nanomaterials [13,14].

Recently, MXene, a new 2D nanomaterial, has attracted extensive attention owing to its rich surface chemistry, superior metallic conductivity, and good mechanical properties, showing huge promises for applications in flexible electronic devices, catalyst, energy storage, aerospace, and water purification [15]. Few-layered MXene nanosheets with large specific surface areas and good flexibility are a promising build-block for fabricating composites used in metallic corrosion protection. Yan et al [16]. reported the first investigated work to confirm that the anticorrosion performance of epoxy (EP) coatings could be greatly improved by few-layered MXene nanosheets. Moreover, it was found that the incorporation of well-dispersed MXene nanosheets could also enhance the antiwear property of EP coatings through lubricating the counterpart/coating interfaces. The good dispersity of MXene nanosheets in coating matrix as well as their high compatibility with EP is important to achieve superior anticorrosion performance of coatings. Chen et al [17]. used silk fibroin modified Ti3C2TX MXene nanosheets (SF-Ti3C2TX) to enhance the compatibility between MXene and EP matrix, and found that the impedance modulus of the SF-Ti3C2TX-EP composite coating with 0.5 wt% SF-Ti3C2TX loading increased by four orders after 240 h of soaking in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Zhao et al [18]. prepared ionic liquid functionalized Ti3C2TX MXene (IL-Ti3C2TX) nanosheets and the anticorrosion performance of IL-Ti3C2TX-EP composite coatings realized a great improvement based on the synergistically effect of IL and Ti3C2TX nanosheets. Yan et al [19]. reported that the amino- and PANI-modified Ti3C2TX-EP composite coatings showed excellent corrosion protection for steels. However, these reported MXene-based composite coatings only presented relatively low impedance modulus (generally < 107 Ω) and their long-term anticorrosion performance was rarely discussed [16-20]. It still remains a huge challenge to design and exploit a simple and effective way to fabricate MXene-based composite coatings with long-lasting anticorrosion performance.

MXene is chemically unstable and tends to oxidation in aqueous, seriously impeding its anticorrosion application. It is reported that MXene is oxidized after only 7 days in aqueous [21]. This inevitably leads to the decomposition of the MXene structure and severely deteriorates the coating properties including integrity, barrier properties, and mechanical properties. Therefore, avoiding oxidation and keeping MXene sheet structure are crucial to preserve the splendid barrier properties and enhanced anticorrosion performance of coatings. In addition, although MXene is similar to graphene with high conductivity [15], up to now, no reports have been made to explore the corrosion-promotion activity of MXene-based coatings. Thus, despite recent progresses, it is of extreme significance to verify the corrosion inhibition of MXene in organic coatings and deeply probe the enhance mechanism for developing high-performance and durable MXnen-based composite coatings.

Carbon dot (CD), a carbon-based nanomaterial, with high biocompatibility, low toxicity, good water solubility, and photoluminescence properties [22]. Cui et al [23]. found that CD could easily absorb on the steel surface to form passive films with excellent corrosion inhibition performance. Zhu et al [24]. reported that CD with great potential as a repairing agent for initiating healing and self-healing in polymers as well as improve anticorrosion performance of coatings. In addition, CD has rich functional groups like hydroxyl and carboxyl, which can act as active sites for covalent functionalization with desired materials [23]. Ye et al [25]. confirmed that CD could enhance the interfacial interactions of graphene and EP, dramatically increasing the anticorrosion and passivation properties of coatings.

Herein, we firstly confirm a facile and efficient strategy to protect sensitive Ti3C2TX MXene plates from oxidation and deterioration through functionalized by CD. Then, we fabricated a self-aligned bioinspired CD-Ti3C2TX-EP composite coating via mixing few-layer CD-Ti3C2TX plates aqueous dispersions in EP matrix, followed by self-assembly in flow-induced process. The CD-Ti3C2TX hybrids and coating systems were characterized through Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectra, UV–vis spectra, transmission electron microscope (TEM), atomic force microscope (AFM), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The corrosion behavior and mechanisms of coatings including EP, bioinspired self-aligned CD-Ti3C2TX-EP (B-CM-EP) and random-dispersed CD-Ti3C2TX-EP (R-CM-EP) were discussed and investigated systemically by electrochemistry and salt spray measurements. The designed B-CM-EP composite coating had superior barrier and passivation properties, and realized long-term corrosion protection for steels. Furthermore, benefiting from the well-designed layered structure, the B-CM-EP coating showed complete inhibition of corrosion-promotion activity at coating defects compared to the R-CM-EP coating counterpart.

Accordion-like Ti3C2TX powder was provided by Ningbo Beijiaer New Material Co. Ltd. Aminosalicylic acid (AA) and ethanol (EtOH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Two-component waterborne EP coatings were provided from Zhejiang Jianhua New Materials Co., Ltd. Q235 steels (1 cm × 1 cm) were purchased from Yangxin Shengxin Technology Co., Ltd. All chemical regents and materials were used without further purification.

CD was synthesized according to previous reports [24]. Specially, 0.5 g of AA was added into 50 ml of EtOH with a 10 min sonication. The AA solution was then transferred into PTFE autoclaves, and kept at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling down to room temperature (25 °C), the dark brown solution was collected. The resulting crude products were dialysed for 1 day (membrane cutoff: 3,000 kDa) in distilled water to remove the un-reacted materials. Black CDs solids were obtained after filtration and drying under vacuum.

The few-layered MXene nanosheets were prepared as previously described works [15,17]. MXene powder was added into 100 ml distilled water and sonicated for 1 h in an ice/water bath with argon protection. The exfoliated few-layered MXene supernatant was obtained after centrifugation at 3500 rpm for 1 h. Furthermore, the CD-Ti3C2TX hybrid was synthesized according to the schematic shown in Fig. 1 a. Briefly, a certain amount of CDs (1:1 of mass ratio) was added into the above few-layered MXene solution and sonicated for 10 min. The mixture solution was then strongly stirred at room temperature for 6 h at 1000 rpm to ensure the fully reaction. Finally, the products were washed completely with distilled water for several times to remove un-reacted materials. After freeze-drying for 24 h, the CD-Ti3C2TX plates were obtained.

The self-aligned composite coating was fabricated as follows. The mass fraction CD-Ti3C2TX in the composite coatings is set 0.5 wt% (based on experimental experience values). 50 ml of CD-Ti3C2TXwater solution (1 mg/ mL) was added to 15 g of waterborne cure regent and stirred and sonicated for 20 min. After removing excess water by a rotate evaporator, 10 g of EP was added and stirred for 10 min. The liquid coating was spraying on the vertically hung substrates and to pre-curing at room temperature; finally the specimen was vertically hung in an oven at 50 °C for complete cured. The process was repeated several times to obtain expected thick coatings, and the sample was rotated 180° at the next cycle of painting to achieve an even layer. The final cured coating sample was marked as B-CM-EP. The blank epoxy coating and random-dispersed composite coating were fabricated with a wire bar coater, and called as EP and R-CM-EP, respectively.

The morphologies of CD, MXene, CD-Ti3C2TX and coating samples were obtained by using TEM (JEOL JEM2100), AFM (VeecoMultiMode/NanoScope IIIa), and SEM (FEI quanta 250). FTIR (Thermo NICOLET6700), XPS (Kratos AXIS ULTRA), ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis, Lambda 950), Raman spectrometer (Super Lab Ram II system), and XRD (BRUKER D8) were performed to evaluate the chemical structure of CDs, Ti3C2TX, CD-Ti3C2TX samples. The FTIR measurements were carried out with the wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1 by using KBr pellets. The XRD analysis of samples was scanned from 5° to 80° for XRD. Raman measurements were conducted at a 532 nm wavelength. The corrosion products generated on the steel surfaces were verified using EDS and Raman mapping.

The corrosion electrochemical measurements of steels protected by different coatings were performed by using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai). The electrochemical experiments are carried out with a typical three electrode system in a 3.5 wt% NaCl buffer (artificial sea water) at room temperature (25 °C). The saturated calomel electrodes acted as reference electrode, the platinum plate (1 × 1 cm2) acted as a counter-electrode, and the coated steels acted as working electrodes. Before testing, the open circuit potential (OCP) was performed for 30 min to keep stable states. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were carried out in a scanning frequency range from 10 MHz to 0.01 Hz at the OCP with 50 mV sinusoidal signal. The Tafel polarization curves (TPCs) were scanned from cathodic to anodic sites (±200 mVOCP) in a 1 mV/s scan rate. In view of the test error and corrosion susceptibility of insulated EP matrix, a ca. 2 mm diameter hole was made by a pinhead to expose the substrate. All tests of each specimen were conducted for three times to ensure the results reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CD-Ti3C2TX composites
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of CD, MXene nanosheets, and CD-Ti3C2TX plates were observed by using TEM images. The fresh exfoliated few-layered MXene nanosheets exhibit clean and tidy feature, in Fig. 1b. Based on the electron diffraction pattern from the inset (Fig. 1b), the hexagonal characteristics of the MXene nanosheets is easily to be discerned, and the corresponding high-resolution TEM image revealing a lattice spacing of 0.2 nm (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. 1c, the as-synthesized CD shows a size range from 15 to 25 nm, which is consistent with the recent reports by Ye et al [25]. After introduction of CD onto MXene nanosheets, the resultant CD-Ti3C2TX composites remains the ultrathin structure of MXenen nanosheets and the CD is uniformly distributed on the surface of MXnen nanosheets, in Fig. 1d. The thicknesses of different nanomaterials were evaluated by AFM measurements. The exfoliated MXene nanosheets have a thickness of ∼ 1.8 nm (Fig. 1e), while the synthesized CD has a thickness of ∼ 2.5 nm (Fig. 1f). The AFM image of the synthesized CD-Ti3C2TX hybrids suggests that the thickness of CD-Ti3C2TX hybrids is 3 ∼ 6 nm (Fig. 1g), which corresponding a thickness sum of CDs and MXene nanosheets. The SEM images were used to further analyze the morphologies of MXene samples. The accordion-like MXene (Fig. 1h) was successfully exfoliated into few-layered MXene nanosheets via ultrasonic treatment (Fig. S2a). The obtained few-layered MXene nanosheets have an average lateral size of ∼ 1.5 μm (Fig. S2b), which is similar to previously reported exfoliated MXene nanosheets [15-17].

In Fig. 1i, heavily agglomerated and decomposition of MXene nanosheets are observed after storage for 5 weeks under environmental conditions, and the typical sheet structure cannot be identified. This indicates that MXene nanosheets have been disintegrated into amorphous TiO2 and disorganized structures in water, which is similar with recently reported researches of MXene materials [18,21]. On the contrary, the modified CD-Ti3C2TX remains perfect sheet morphologies after aging of 5 weeks, in Fig. 1j. In addition, after aging for 5 weeks at room temperature, the CD-Ti3C2TX water dispersion retains dark color with a typical Dindal effect (the inset in Fig. 1j), however, the MXene dispersion has become cloudy white (the inset in Fig. 1i), which directly confirms that the CD-Ti3C2TX possesses high chemical stability to postpone the degradation of MXene in water.

In Fig. 2 a, the CD and MXene in CD-Ti3C2TX hybrids can form Ti-O-C covalent bonding through nucleophilic substitution and dehydration reactions [15]. Due to the abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups functional in CD, which can generate C = OH+ and -C-OH2 + with H+ ions in water [25,26]. At the same time, the Ti-O-in MXene reacts with the C = OH+ and –C-OH2 + to form Ti-O-C covalent bonding after losing water molecules [15]. The Ti-O-C covalent bonding in CD-Ti3C2TX is critical for holding the 2D sheet architecture of MXene, which can block MXene oxidation and decomposition.

The chemical structures of CD-Ti3C2TX were characterized in detailed via FTIR, XPS, UV–vis, Raman, and XRD tests. In Fig. 2b, the CD displays typical FTIR peaks at 1255, 1617, 1726 cm−1, belonging to the C-O stretching vibration [22], NH2 deformation vibration, and C = O stretching vibration, respectively. The peak at ∼ 3360 cm−1 is attributed to the O–H and N–H groups [27]. For MXene nanosheets, the peaks at 1395 cm−1 and 1103 cm−1 can be assigned to the C-F stretching vibration and the O–H bending vibration [28], respectively. The peaks around 3400 cm−1 and 1660 cm−1 are assigned to the O–H stretching vibration because of the absorbed water in MXene [29]. After modification by CD, new peaks at 840 cm−1 and 1660 cm−1 in CD-Ti3C2TX are detected, implying the formation of Ti-O-C and C = O bonding.

The reactions between CD and MXene could be also verified by XPS spectra. It can be seen from Ti 2p spectra in Fig. 2c, MXene presents four typical absorptions at 455.1, 458.5, 461.4, and 464.2 eV, corresponding to the Ti-C 2p3/2, Ti-O 2p3/2, Ti-C 2p1/2, and Ti-O 2p1/2 bonds, respectively [30]. However, the Ti-O 2p peak in CD-Ti3C2TX slightly shifts from 458.5 to 458.7 eV as well as from 464.2 to 464.8 eV. Besides, an additional absorption belongs to Ti-O-C bonds is observed at 456.9 eV. The C 1 s spectra of CD and CD-Ti3C2TX are illustrated in Fig. 2d. The MXene exhibits six characteristic peaks at 279.2, 283.2, 283.5, 283.9, 285.8, and 288.0 eV, which can be ascribed to C-Ti, C = C, C–C, C-O, C-F, and O-C = O bonds, respectively [27]. After reaction with CD, a new C-N bond peak appears at 284.1 eV is found. Additionally, the C-O and O-C = O atomic percentages growth from to 25.6% and 11.2 % for the CD-Ti3C2TX composites (from 18.3% and 6.4% for MXene) after modification. This further reveals the chemical reaction of MXene with CD.

The UV–vis spectra were applied to verify the interactions between CD and MXene in CD-Ti3C2TX composites. In Fig. 2e, the CD displays two characteristic absorptions at 206 nm and 330 nm, assigning to the π-π* transition of C = C and the n-π* transition of carboxyl groups, respectively [31]. However, the characteristic absorption at 206 nm for CD blue-shifts to 202 nm for CD-Ti3C2TX, meanwhile the intensity of the characteristic absorption around 330 nm weakens. UV–vis results suggested that there are π-π interactions between CD and MXene nanosheets.

Raman spectra were employed to probe the crystals structure characteristics of CD-Ti3C2TX, in Fig. 2f. Generally, the MXene shows a characteristic A1g symmetry out-of-plane vibration (719 cm−1) and E g group in-plane vibrations (at 284, 371, and 625 cm−1) of Ti, C and surface functional group atoms [15]. As expected, these vibrations are detected in the exfoliated few-layered MXene nanosheets. The CD displays a G peak at 1585 cm−1 and a D peak at 1350 cm−1 (Fig. S3), which are well agreed with previously reported carbon-based nanomaterials like graphene [32,33]. The G peak can reflect the crystallinity of CD, whereas the D peak indicates the defects and disorder. It can be seen that the ratio of ID/IG increases from 0.91 for CD to 1.1 for CD-Ti3C2TX due to the covalent reaction between CD and MXene [17]. Moreover, the G peak is shifted from 1585 cm−1 for CD to 1581 cm−1 for CD-Ti3C2TX, implying a charge transfer exists between CD and MXene. Raman results are well consist with the FTIR, XPS, and UV–vis measurements.

The XRD patterns of MXene nanosheets and CD-Ti3C2TX composites are shown in Fig. 2g. A typical 2θ peak around 7.02° is detected in the MXene nanosheets, which confirms the exfoliated MXene have a 12.86 Å d spacing and demonstrates that the MXene nanosheets have been completely exfoliated [15,17]. The d-spacing of the CD-Ti3C2TX increases to 7.56 Å, indicating that the CD has been successfully inserted into the interlamellars of MXene nanosheets [33]. In addition, the tested electrical conductivities of MXene and CD-Ti3C2TX are 1350 ± 100 and 1400 ± 100 S/cm, respectively. This indicates that the CD plays a positive impact on the electrical conductivity of MXene nanosheets.

To investigate the distribution and compatibility between CD-Ti3C2TX and EP, the cross-sectional SEM images of coatings were obtained. In Fig. 2h, owing to the rapid water evaporation during the cured process, the neat EP coating fracture surface exhibits characteristics of multiple pinholes and defects. After embedding self-aligned CD-Ti3C2TX plates in EP matrix, the B-CM-EP bioinspired coating shows a uniform and denser fracture surface, and the defects disappear in the coating matrix, in Fig. 2i. This demonstrates that the self-aligned CD-Ti3C2TX plates can significantly enhance their compatibility with EP, and reduced the water evaporation rate of coating matrix. In case of the R-CM-EP coating, the CD-Ti3C2TX plates display good dispersion overall, however, the fracture surface is relatively rough compared to the B-CM-EP coating, suggesting its disordered distribution and random orientation feature.

In order to accurately analyze the dispersion states of CD-Ti3C2TX plates in EP coatings, we carried out TEM measurements through the ultrathin sections of the coatings. Fig. 2k-m illustrates the cross-sectional TEM images of coatings, which allows us to detect the distribution and orientation of CD-Ti3C2TX plates in B-CM-EP and R-CM-EP composite coatings at the nanometer scale. It is noted that the bright areas represent the EP matrix, while the dark lines represent the CD-Ti3C2TX plates in the TEM images. In Fig. 2l, the self-aligned and parallel arranged CD-Ti3C2TX plates are observed in B-CM-EP coating. The layered arrangement of B-CM-EP is easily reminiscent of the hierarchical structure of natural nacre (the inset). This implies that CD-Ti3C2TX plates can achieve self-align and parallel orientation in the EP matrix under flow induction (Fig. S4a), and facilitates the improvement of the physical barrier effect and anticorrosion performance of the B-CM-EP coating [32,34]. For the R-CM-EP coating, CD-Ti3C2TX plates behavior a homogeneous distribution overall, however, which is not well aligned and with a relatively large sheet curvature to form interconnections of structure, in Fig. 2m. In this work, the main driving force of self-assembly for B-CM-EP system is the flow induction. The B-CM-EP coating was hung vertically so that the CD-Ti3C2TX plates can be self-aligned with the aid of the flow of a thin liquid layer induced by the gravity (Fig. S4a). At the same time, during the drying of coating, to accommodate neighboring plates the crowded CD-Ti3C2TX plates also impel themselves to keep high orientation [35]. Moreover, the curing processes further benefits the self-alignment of CD-Ti3C2TX plates [36]. The R-CM-EP coating was fabricated by horizontally casting. Due to the initial flow induction tends to be zero (Fig. S4b), the CD-Ti3C2TX plates present irregular dispersion and random orientation in the coating matrix during the curing process. The ordered aligned CD-Ti3C2TX plates possess larger effective aspect ratio and projected area than that of random ones [34,35], thus, which is expected bring a higher physical barrier and anticorrosion properties.

The thickness of coatings was measured by SEM measurements, the samples were dipped into liquid nitrogen and then broken for exposure the fracture surfaces. In Fig. 3 a-c, the estimated thicknesses of coatings are 26.1 μm for EP, 24.9 μm for R-CM-EP, and 24.7 μm for B-CM-EP, respectively, indicating their ultrathin characteristics. Furthermore, the water contact angles of the coatings were measured, as illustrated in the insets in Fig. 3a-c. Typically, a coating with larger contact angle will better against the invasion of aggressive species [37]. The pure EP coating exhibits a relatively low contact angle of 70.3°. When CD-Ti3C2TX plates were embed into the EP matrix, the contact angles of composite coatings increase to 91.5° for R-CM-EP and 99.7° for B-CM-EP. Results demonstrate that the self-aligned CD-Ti3C2TX plates are more effective and efficient in enhancing the antipermeation performance of EP coatings based on its higher dispersity and compatible properties (Fig. 2i and l).

It is well known that OCP is a universal technique to evaluate the corrosion protection properties of coatings [24]. The OCP values of steels protected by different coatings were recorded at various immersion period intervals in 3.5% NaCl solution, in Fig. S5. It can be seen that all the coatings have a high OCP value at the initial stage of immersion, revealing their satisfactory anticorrosion performance. With increasing immersion time, the OCP values of EP coating reduce from the largest value of −0.17 V vs. SCE to the smallest value of −0.58 V vs. SCE owing to the fast invasion of aggressive species, suggesting the rapid deterioration of EP coating in corrosion medium. Compared to EP coating, both the R-CM-EP and B-CM-EP coatings display significantly increased protection performance during the soaking periods. Despite the R-CM-EP presents gradually downward OCP values, but which is still higher than EP, revealing random-dispersed CD-Ti3C2TX plates could defer the penetration of aggressive species. In contrast, during the whole service cycle, the OCP values of B-CM-EP slightly decline from −0.08 V vs. SCE to −0.22 V vs. SCE, confirming that the self-aligned CD-Ti3C2TX could endow the coating super barrier capability and corrosion protective performance in corrosion medium. The OCP measures are well consist with contact angle results.

The EIS was applied to further evaluate the anticorrosion performance of EP and composite coatings. Fig. 3d-i presents the Bode modulus and Bode phase plots of steels protected by different coating types. In this study, the impedance modulus (|Z|0.01Hz) at the lowest frequency of 0.01 Hz was employed to compare the protection ability of coatings, and a higher |Z|0.01Hz value reflects better protection performance [38,39]. In Fig. 3d, the EP coating has a relatively lower |Z|0.01Hz value of 1.1 × 108 Ω at the initial immersion stage. After 35 days of immersion, the |Z|0.01Hz value fell sharply to 6.7 × 105 Ω, which is drooped by nearly 3 orders of magnitude, demonstrating that the pure EP coating has the poor barrier ability and protection performance. In this case, the invasion channels of aggressive species have been opened and the EP coating has been failed [27]. The R-CM-EP presents a similar reduce trend (Fig. 3e), but the |Z|0.01Hz values (from 3.4 × 109 Ω to 1.1 × 107 Ω) is much higher than EP coating in the whole immersion time, implying that the CD-Ti3C2TX plates could delay the diffusion of aggressive species in coating matrix through the “maze” effects derived from zigzag paths. As expected, during the whole 35 days of immersion, the B-CM-EP coating possesses the highest |Z|0.01Hz (from 4.1 × 1010 Ω to 5.1 × 109 Ω) in Fig. 3f, which is 4 and 2 orders of magnitude higher than EP and R-CM-EP coatings, respectively, revealing the B-CM-EP could endow the best corrosion protection for steels. The Bode phase plots in Fig. 3g-i allow us to detect the failure process of coatings [40]. Obviously, the resistive response of all the coatings in Bode plots occupies a wider frequency range with increasing of immersion time, indicating that aggressive species in corrosion medium expedite the failure of coating matrix. In Bode phase plots, the high-frequencies (105 ∼ 103 Hz) time constant indicates the coating response (reflects the capacitance characteristics), the middle-frequencies (103 ∼ 100 Hz) time constant indicates the defect response of coatings, and the low-frequencies (100 ∼ 10-2 Hz) time constant indicates the corrosion response of steels [41–43]. In Fig. 3g, after 35 days of immersion, the EP coating presents two time constants, and a large corrosion response peak is detected at 100 ∼ 10-2 Hz, confirming that the aggressive species have contacted the substrate and leaded to steel corrosion [39]. In the case of R-CM-EP (Fig. 3h), the Bode phase plots also have two time constants after 35 days, however, compared with EP, the additional time only reflects the defect response of coatings at 103 ∼ 100 Hz, which implies the corrosion was effectively delayed. Evidently, the R-CM-EP coating always exhibits one time constant during the whole immersion periods (Fig. 3i), suggesting that the aggressive species still keep some distance away from the coating/substrate interfaces. Additionally, the high-frequency phase angles of composite coatings especially B-CM-EP coating are closed to 90◦, verifying that the steels protected by composite coatings no or only slight corrode. Bode-phase plots further confirmed that the introduction of CD-Ti3C2TX could prolong greatly the failure of EP coatings. According to EIS data, we can conclude that the B-CM-EP has the best corrosion protection for steels among the three coatings, which is ascribed to that the self-aligned parallel arrangement CD-Ti3C2TX provide optimized physical barrier property.

To deeply investigate the corrosion behaviors of the coatings, the EIS results were fitted through corresponding equivalent circuit models R(Q(R)) (Fig. S6a) and R(Q(R(Q(R))) (Fig. S6b) by using Zsimpwin software. For the circuit models, R s represents the solution resistance, R c and Q c represent the coating resistance and coating capacitance, and R ct and Qdl represent the charge transfer resistance and double-layer capacitance, respectively. The R(Q(R)) model is suitable to fit the coatings before corrosion reaction, including pure EP coating for 1 day of immersion, R-CM-EP coating for 15 days of immersion, and B-CM-EP coating. When the aggressive species reached the coating/metal interface (pure EP for 10 days of immersion and R-CM-EP coating for 25 days of immersion) even corrosion reaction occurred (pure EP for 35 days of immersion), the R(Q(R(Q(R))) model can be applied [40]. Fig. 3j shows the fitting R c results, and the detailed parameters were summarized in Table S1. In general, the R c values of coatings decline with the immersion time, which can imply the corrosion states of steels, and the larger the R c value, the better the anticorrosion performance [41,44]. From Fig. 3j, the R c values of EP coating sharply reduce during the 35 days of immersion. From 1 day to 15 days, the R c values gradually drop from 1.2 × 108 Ω to 5.8 × 106 Ω, which can be attributed to that the aggressive species have entered the corrosion channels and reached the interface of coating/substrate at this immersion stage. Subsequently, the R c values continues to fall from 1.7 × 106 Ω for 25 days immersion to 6.3 × 105 Ω for 35 days immersion. This indicates that the EP coating has lost its protection ability for steel owing to the large-scale accumulation of corrosion products, pinholes or cracks. Compared to EP coating, however, the composite coatings exhibit a slower resistance decrease, suggesting an effectively reduced penetration rate of aggressive species. It can be seen that the R c values of R-CM-EP coating decline from 2.6 × 109 Ω to 1.3 × 107 Ω and after 35 days of soaking. However, the B-CM-EP still remains a relatively high R c value of 5.1 × 109 Ω after 35 days of immersion. The R c results verify that the self-assembled and parallel arrangement CD-Ti3C2TX plates are more efficient on improving the barrier and anticorrosion performance of EP coatings than random dispersion ones.

The R c of B-CM-EP coating was compared with other similar studies in, graphene-, BN-, and MXene-based composite coatings, as shown in Fig. 3k (the detailed parameters are summarized in Table S2). [16,18-20,45-53] The R c values of the reported 2D composite coatings range from ∼ 105 Ω to ∼ 108 Ω because of the poor distribution and disordered oriented structure. The CD-Ti3C2TX plates in the B-CM-EP coating, perpendicular to the diffused direction of aggressive species, presents extremely high barrier properties, reaching a extraordinary R c value that is 4 orders of magnitude much higher that of EP coating. Surprisingly, the R c value of the B-CM-EP is 2 ∼ 3 orders higher than those of other reported MXene- even graphene- and BN-based composite coatings [16,19,20,53]. In view of R c results, our B-CM-EP coating is superior to other forms of 2D composite coatings.

To investigate the influence of the arrangement structure of CD-Ti3C2TX plates on the antipermeation and anticorrosion performance of coatings, the water-uptake (Wup) of coatings was studied according to the Brasher − Kingsbury formula [35].

are the coating capacitance at initial and t times, respectively. The Wup data are depicted in Fig. 4 a. It can be seen that the introduction of CD-Ti3C2TX plates can dramatically decrease the water absorption of EP coatings. As expected, the self-assembly B-CM-EP has the lowest Wup value suggesting it possesses incomparable antipermeation property [54-56]. The extremely low Wup value of B-CM-EP is benefited from the parallel arrangement of CD-Ti3C2TX plates, which can maximize the barrier performance of coatings through prolonging the diffusion pathways for aggressive species [57,58], thus significantly enhancing the anticorrosion performance.

In addition, the breakpoint frequency (f b, frequency at 45° phase angle) can be applied to evaluate the deterioration degree of coatings [23], and the larger of the f b is, the more serious the damaged regions at the interface of coating/substrate are. The change of f b for the coatings as a function of immersion time is illustrated in Fig. 4b. The f b values of all the coatings increase with the immersion time, indicating the gradual deterioration of the coatings matrix. Especially, the pure EP coating exhibits the highest f b value during the whole service periods, revealing it has the worst corrosion protection for steels. However, the raise of f b values for the R-CM-EP and B-CM-EP coatings are much smaller compared to EP, indicating their significantly improved damage resistance. In addition, the extremely low f b value of B-CM-EP confirms its exceptional corrosion protection for steels.

Raman spectra can be applied to study the composition of corrosion products and reveal the electrochemical reaction types on steel surfaces, in Fig. 4c. It can be found that the steel surface protected by EP coating presents typical Raman activity and covering many corrosion products (Fig. 4d). Three characteristic peaks are observed in EP sample, which are ascribed to assigned FeOOH (393 and 1310 cm−1), Fe2O3 (285 cm−1), and Fe3O4 (542 and 668 cm−1) vibration modes of steel corrosion products [35,39]. Two characteristic peaks related to the Fe2O3 and Fe3O4 are detected in the R-CM-EP sample, and the steel surface suffered from slightly corrodes (Fig. 4e). However, only a small Fe3O4 characteristic peak is detected in the B-CM-EP sample, and no obvious corrosion product is observed on the steel surface (Fig. 4f).

The difference of corrosion product types between EP and composite coatings can be attributed to the variation of water and oxygen content participating in the corrosion reactions. For the pure EP, the electrochemical corrosion reactions occur on the steel surfaces exhibit active states due to its poor barrier performance (Fig. 4a) lead to high availability of water, oxygen, and chloride ions, so that the corrosion products were mainly transformed into Fe(OH)2 and a little of Fe2O3 (Fig. 4g). For the composite coating systems, owing to the high barrier performance, the further electrochemical reactions of steel surfaces can be efficiently suppressed under the low availability of water, oxygen, and chloride ions, and the Fe(OH)2 and Fe(OH)3 were transformed into nonconductive Fe2O3 and/or Fe3O4 films after loss of water, finally the steels remained a passive sates due to lacking of electron sink. The EDS results reveal that the EP sample has a high total oxygen element of 14.9 wt% (inset in Fig. 4d), however, only 3.4 wt% and 1.5 wt% of oxygen element are detected on the R-CM-EP and B-CM-EP specimens, implying that the addition of well-dispersed CD-Ti3C2TX in EP matrix can greatly block the permeation of oxygen in coatings and the oxidation of steels, and enhance the barrier and anticorrosion performance of coatings. The Raman mapping can detail verify the electrochemical corrosion process of EP, R-CM-EP, and B-CM-EP systems. For the pure EP coating, from Fig. 4d1-d3, the corrosion products are main compose of FeOOH as well as a little of Fe2O3 and Fe3O4, which corresponds to the electrochemical corrosion process in Fig. 4g (1). In the case of the R-CM-EP, it shows a converse corrosion states to EP (Fig. 4e1-de3), which is due to that the presence of CD-Ti3C2TX could improve the barrier property and block the further infiltration of aggressive specials into coating matrix, and the iron was mainly oxidized into Fe2O3 and Fe3O4. The R-CM-EP presents a similar Raman mapping to R-CM-EP, in Fig. 4f1-df3. However, due to the ordered distribution and orientation of CD-Ti3C2TX in coating matrix, which can provide stronger physical barrier effect than the R-CM-EP. Therefore, fewer corrosion products are formed on the B-CM-EP protected steel surface, and unlike R-CM-EP case, the iron was mainly oxidized into Fe3O4. Both R-CM-EP and B-CM-EP Raman mapping reflect the hypothetical corrosion mechanism in Fig. 4g (2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Passivation effect and corrosion promotion activity.
ce:section.ce:para: The corrosion behaviors of EP, R-CM-EP, and B-CM-EP scratched coatings with artificial 2 mm defects on steel surfaces were studied through TPCs. In TPCs a more positive corrosion potential (E corr) indicates a lower corrosion probability and the corrosion current density (I corr) is the corrosion rate measure [23]. Fig. 5 a-c illustrates the corresponding TPCs of scratched coatings immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at different stages. Compared to EP, the E corr of steels beneath B-CM-EP positively shifts, while the I corr becomes more negative, meaning the B-CM-EP coating decreases both the corrosion odds and the corrosion rate of steels. For EP and B-CM-EP samples, TPCs results agree well with the EIS measurements. However, the TPCs also confirm that the corrosion resistance of the random-dispersed R-CM-EP is even worse than that of pure EP, which cut across the EIS results. A possible reason is that the randomly orientated CD-Ti3C2TX in the coating matrix form MXene/MXene and MXene-metal connections, through which an accelerated current created from electrochemical reactions at the coating defects was captured [54], which may promote corrosion of steel.

Fig. 5c and d show the E corr and the I corr of steel electrodes protected by scratched coatings during the 2 days of immersion. It can be seen that both the negative E corr and the I corr are declined of scratched B-CM-EP coating with the increase of immersion time, which can be attributed the corrosion passivation effect of CD in CD-Ti3C2TX. This passivation process of CD was also confirmed by the similar works previously reported [23-25]. However, such a phenomenon is not detected in the case of scratched EP coating, and after immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl, the negative E corr and I corr increased indicating the gradual corrosion. Besides, due to the passivation effect and corrosion promotion activity of CD-Ti3C2TX compete with each other, and the corrosion-promotion effect of CD-Ti3C2TX appears during the service. As a result, the anticorrosion performance of scratched R-CM-EP coating is even lower than the scratched pure EP. However, due to the self-aligned arrangement of CD-Ti3C2TX plates in the B-CM-EP coating with a space isolation structure perpendicular to coating direction, thus, the coating completely inhibit the corrosion-promotion activity.

The scratched coatings corrosion behaviors were also studied by EIS tests after 2 days of immersion in3.5 wt% NaCl. As presented in Fig. 5f, the Nyquist spectra of all the coatings are compose of two time constants. The fitting results of EIS plots reveals that the charge transfer resistance R ct values of scratched EP coatings are 170 KΩ, while 3306 KΩ for the scratched B-CM-EP coatings, which suggests that the accumulation of electric charges at the interface of B-CM-EP/substrate is hindered. This is mainly due to the passivation effect of CD reduces the corrosion activity of the substrate. However, for the scratched R-CM-EP coatings, the charge transfer resistance dropped to 29 KΩ, indicating the increased corrosion activity of the substrate. Both the scratched EIS and TPCs tests reveal that the scratched B-CM-EP had an excellent passivation property and corrosion-inhibition, while the scratched R-CM-EP exhibited typical corrosion-promotion activity at the coating defects.

SEM was used to characterize the corrosion morphologies of scratched samples to support the electrochemical results. In Fig. 5g, corrosion products are observed in the scratch of EP coating protected steel, and the scratch becomes relatively crude. Obviously, more serious corrosion occurred and more corrosion products were found in the scratch of R-CM-EP covered steel due to the corrosion promotion effect of random-dispersed CD-Ti3C2TX plates in coating matrix, in Fig. 5h. However, no visible corrosion products are detected in the scratch of B-CM-EP coated steel (Fig. 5i), which is even smooth as ever. SEM results are well agreed with the scratched EIS and TPCs experiments.

Finally, plausible mechanisms were proposed by which can explain the passivation effects and corrosion inhibition/promotion of coatings (Fig. 5j-l). The EP coating has poor physical barrier property, which is not enough to prevent the expansion of micropores/capillaries in coating matrix, and lead to the formation of coating defects and damages through which the aggressive species corrode the steel substrate (Fig. 5j). For the case of R-CM-EP, although the CD-Ti3C2TX plates have enhanced barrier properties for the coating matrix, however, considering the high conductivity of CD-Ti3C2TX, the MXene-steel connections can accelerate the galvanic corrosion at the coating defects (Fig. 5k). Therefore, the scratched R-CM-EP presents worse anticorrosion performance than EP. By parallel orientation arrangement, the CD-Ti3C2TX plates not only can fully exert their barrier properties, but also successfully cut off the connections of MXene-MXene/MXene-steel perpendicular to the direction of permeation of aggressive species and electrodes for completely inhibiting the corrosion-promotion activity (Fig. 5l). At the same time, under the coordination bonding between the heteroatom of CDs and steel, the CDs could be well adsorbed on the exposed surfaces of steel to play superior corrosion inhibition effects, thus which effectively obstruct corrosion ions erode and to inhibit the expansion of defects. Moreover, the CD can also adsorb water and trap oxygen through its own hydrophilic functional groups and thus inhibit the corrosion process [23]. Therefore, MXene nanosheets and CD are mutually beneficial and play an important synergistic role in improving performance of B-CM-EP coatings and suppressing the corrosion of steels.

Neutral salt spray tests were carried out to further evaluate the long-term anticorrosion properties of the composite coatings. For the tests, the scratched coating samples were placed in a spray chamber with continuous salt fog of 5 wt% NaCl at 35 °C. Fig. 6 displays the digital photos of the coating samples after salt spray experiment for 0, 50, 100, and 200 h. It can be observed that the corrosive degrees of all the steels protected by different coatings gradually progressed with the time prolonged. After 50 h of exposure, lots of tan corrosion products generated in the scratches of EP and R-CM-EP samples, especially for the R-CM-EP the rust has fully filled the scratch, whereas the corrosion products are almost negligible for the B-CM-EP sample. With the time increases 100 h, the corrosion of EP and R-CM-EP became more serious, and the corrosion degree of R-CM-EP is much higher than the EP due to the corrosion-promotion activity. In terms of the B-CM-EP coating, the defects were slightly corroded. After 200 h of exposure, the scratches are completely covered by the corrosion products for the EP and R-CM-EP samples, and the corrosion areas for R-CM-EP is wider than EP, revealing the worse anticorrosion performance. In contrast, less corrosion products are found in on the defects of B-CM-EP sample after 200 h of salt spray tests, which suggests the steel only suffered from slightly corrode. According to the neutral salt spray test, we can conclude that the coating structures play vital influence on the anticorrosion performance of the composite coatings, and the self-aligned B-CM-EP coating has much better protection for steels than the random-dispersed R-CM-EP coating. The salt spray tests well supported electrochemical and scratched experimental results.

In summary, a new of CD-Ti3C2Tx MXene hybrid was synthesized through covalent functionalization of MXene nanosheets by CD, which not only showed highly chemical stability in water to remain the 2D sheets structure, but also superior dispersity and compatibility with EP. Insights into the microstructure, corrosion behaviors, passivation properties, and corrosion-promotion activity of EP coatings reinforced by CD-Ti3C2Tx plates were systematically studied, based on the flow-induced self-aligned B-CM-EP and rolling-coated random-dispersed R-CM-EP composite coatings. Experimental results demonstrated that the self-aligned parallel assembly CD-Ti3C2Tx plates could greatly enhance the barrier and anticorrosion properties of B-CM-EP coating and endow an additional passivation effect. After 35 days soaking in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, the impedance modulus of B-CM-EP are 4 orders of magnitude higher than the pure EP, and 2 orders of R-CM-EP, revealing the B-CM-EP could provide long-term protection for steels. Furthermore, the reliability of the flow-induced assembly B-CM-EP coatings in the inhibition of corrosion-promotion activity was also studied. This work develops an efficient protection strategy for MXene nanosheets, and also proves the possibility of potential applications of MXene materials in high-performance anticorrosion fields.
